sections.heading: More mothers and caretakers said physical punishment is needed
sections.body: In 2025, 18.3 percent of mothers and caretakers said a child needs to be physically punished, up from 11.9 percent in 2022. This page measures what people said they believe, not how they actually discipline children, so the change should be read as a shift in stated belief. Even with that caution, the 6.4-point increase is the main national pattern in the data.

sections.heading: CAR and Northern Mindanao had the highest support
sections.body: Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR) had the highest level in 2025: 42.6 percent of mothers and caretakers said physical punishment is needed. Northern Mindanao followed at 36.6 percent, and Davao Region was also high at 29.0 percent. All three were far above the national level of 18.3 percent.

sections.heading: The lowest regions were near 10 percent or below
sections.body: Central Visayas had the lowest level in 2025 at 7.7 percent. Ilocos Region followed at 8.0 percent, and Cagayan Valley was at 11.3 percent. CAR was 34.9 percentage points higher than Central Visayas. That regional gap is much wider than most differences among the nonregional groups.

sections.heading: Most regions rose between 2022 and 2025
sections.body: The national increase also showed up in many regions. CAR rose from 16.7 percent in 2022 to 42.6 percent in 2025. Northern Mindanao rose from 19.9 to 36.6 percent, and Davao Region rose from 15.1 to 29.0 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ also increased, from 20.0 to 25.4 percent. These changes suggest a broad rise in stated acceptance, not a shift limited to one area.

sections.heading: Some regions did not follow the national rise
sections.body: Not every region increased. Central Visayas fell from 9.8 percent in 2022 to 7.7 percent in 2025, and Ilocos Region dipped from 8.6 to 8.0 percent. BARMM changed only slightly, from 13.7 to 15.1 percent. These exceptions show that the national rise did not remove low-attitude regions or create the same pattern everywhere.

sections.heading: Men were slightly more likely to support physical punishment in 2025
sections.body: In 2025, 20.6 percent of male respondents said physical punishment is needed, compared with 17.6 percent of female respondents. The increase since 2022 was larger among males, who rose from 10.9 percent, while females rose from 12.2 percent. The sex gap in 2025 is modest, but it reverses the 2022 ordering.

sections.heading: Urban and rural respondents had the same level in 2025
sections.body: Urban and rural respondents were both at 18.3 percent in 2025. In 2022, the urban level was 12.6 percent and the rural level was 11.3 percent, so both groups increased by 2025. Unlike the regional results, place of residence does not split the 2025 attitude pattern.

sections.heading: Support was highest among respondents aged 25 to 34
sections.body: Respondents aged 25 to 34 had the highest level in 2025 at 20.9 percent. The other age groups were lower and close together: 16.5 percent among those younger than 25, 18.0 percent among those aged 35 to 49, and 16.6 percent among those aged 50 or older. All age groups increased from 2022, with the largest rise among those aged 25 to 34.

sections.heading: Education levels did not follow a simple pattern
sections.body: By mother's educational attainment, the highest 2025 level was in the post-secondary group at 27.0 percent. Grades 1 to 6 followed at 20.6 percent, and college was at 19.5 percent. Grades 7 to 10 stood at 17.4 percent, while the no-education group was 16.6 percent. The pattern does not show that higher education is consistently linked with lower acceptance of physical punishment.

sections.heading: Wealth differences existed, but they were uneven
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile had a 2025 level of 20.1 percent, while the highest quintile was at 16.1 percent. The middle quintile was also relatively low at 16.2 percent, but the fourth quintile was 19.9 percent, close to the lowest quintile. This points to some wealth-related difference, but not a smooth step-by-step pattern across all quintiles.

takeaways: Nationwide, the share who said a child needs to be physically punished rose from 11.9 percent in 2022 to 18.3 percent in 2025.
Regional differences were large in 2025, ranging from CAR at 42.6 percent to Central Visayas at 7.7 percent.
Residence did not separate the 2025 pattern, while sex, age, education, and wealth showed smaller and less consistent differences.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
